content.text: Platform vs Application Landing Zones

An Azure landing zone separates shared infrastructure concerns from workload-specific concerns by splitting them into two distinct types: platform landing zones, which contain the centralized networking, identity, and management services that all workloads consume; and application landing zones, which are isolated subscriptions where individual workloads deploy their own resources. This division is the organizational foundation of the Cloud Adoption Framework's Enterprise-Scale architecture.

attrs.alt: Azure landing zone architecture — platform subscriptions vs application spoke subscriptions

content.text: The platform team provisions the application landing zone (the subscription, its spoke VNet, and VNet peering to the hub) and then hands it to the application team. The application team deploys within it. This creates a clean separation of responsibility with no shared state inside a subscription.
What a Platform Landing Zone Contains

A platform landing zone is a set of subscriptions managed by the central platform team that provide shared services to all application landing zones. The three canonical platform subscriptions are: connectivity (hub VNet, Azure Firewall, ExpressRoute/VPN, DNS), identity (Active Directory Domain Services, Entra ID Connect, Privileged Access Management), and management (Log Analytics workspace, Update Manager, policy remediation automation).
Platform subscription
Core resources
Owned by
Cost model
sub-connectivity-prod
Hub VNet, Azure Firewall, VPN/ExpressRoute gateway, Private DNS zones, Bastion
Platform / Networking team
Shared cost allocated to consumers via chargeback
sub-identity-prod
AD DS VMs, Entra ID Connect, Azure AD Domain Services, certificates
Identity / Security team
Shared cost allocated proportionally
sub-management-prod
Log Analytics workspace, Automation Account, Azure Monitor, Update Manager
Platform / Operations team
Shared cost allocated proportionally
What an Application Landing Zone Contains

An application landing zone is a subscription that hosts the resources for one workload or one business unit. It contains a spoke VNet peered to the hub, workload-specific resource groups (app, data, network), NSGs, private endpoints to shared platform services, and RBAC assignments that grant the application team access to their subscription. The application team has full Contributor access within the subscription but cannot modify platform-level resources or the hub VNet.
Aspect
Application team responsibility
Platform team responsibility
Networking
Spoke VNet subnets, NSGs, private endpoints, route table UDRs
Hub VNet, Firewall, DNS zones, VNet peering creation
Identity
Managed identities for their apps, RBAC within their subscription
AD DS, Entra ID tenant, service principal lifecycle
Monitoring
Application-specific alerts, Application Insights instances
Central Log Analytics workspace, workspace RBAC, diagnostics routing
Policy
Compliance within the policies assigned at MG/subscription level
Policy definitions and assignments at management group scope
Cost
Cost of resources within their subscription
Cost of shared platform services; chargeback allocation
Subscription lifecycle
None — they consume it
Create, name, move into management group, configure initial RBAC
Provisioning a Platform Landing Zone

Platform landing zone provisioning is a one-time operation performed by the platform team. It creates the hub VNet, Azure Firewall, private DNS zones for all Azure service private endpoints, and links the DNS zones to the hub VNet. In real deployments this is done via Bicep or Terraform modules from the ALZ accelerator; the bash below shows the equivalent CLI steps for understanding.

# Platform landing zone: provision the hub subscription resources
# This is run once by the platform team for the entire organization.

PLATFORM_SUB='<connectivity-sub-id>'
az account set --subscription $PLATFORM_SUB

# 1. Create the connectivity resource groups
az group create --name rg-connectivity-prod-eastus --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=prod Application=connectivity CostCenter=cc-platform Owner=platform-team@contoso.com

# 2. Create the hub VNet
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefixes 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --subnet-name snet-gateway-prod-eastus \
  --subnet-prefixes 10.0.255.0/27

# 3. Add the Azure Firewall subnet (must be named exactly 'AzureFirewallSubnet')
az network vnet subnet create \
  --name AzureFirewallSubnet \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.0.0.0/26

# 4. Create a public IP for the Firewall
az network public-ip create \
  --name pip-afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --sku Standard --zone 1 2 3

# 5. Deploy Azure Firewall
az network firewall create \
  --name afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --location eastus \
  --sku-name AZFW_VNet \
  --sku-tier Standard

az network firewall ip-config create \
  --firewall-name afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --name FW-config \
  --public-ip-address pip-afw-hub-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus

# 6. Create private DNS zones for Azure services
for ZONE in privatelink.blob.core.windows.net privatelink.vaultcore.azure.net privatelink.database.windows.net; do
  az network private-dns zone create \
    --name $ZONE \
    --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus

  az network private-dns link vnet create \
    --name "link-hub-${ZONE//./-}" \
    --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
    --zone-name $ZONE \
    --virtual-network vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
    --registration-enabled false
done
Provisioning an Application Landing Zone

An application landing zone is provisioned by the platform team at the request of an application team. The provisioning script creates the spoke VNet, standard subnet layout (app + data), and the two peering connections (hub-to-spoke and spoke-to-hub). It ends by granting the application team Contributor on the subscription. After this handoff, the application team deploys their workload resources into the spoke subscription independently.

# Application landing zone: provision a spoke subscription for a workload
# Run once per workload by the platform team; then handed to the app team.

WORKLOAD='payments'
APP_SUB='<payments-sub-id>'
HUB_SUB='<connectivity-sub-id>'
HUB_VNET_ID="/subscriptions/$HUB_SUB/resourceGroups/rg-connectivity-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.Network/virtualNetworks/vnet-hub-prod-eastus"

az account set --subscription $APP_SUB

# 1. Create resource groups
az group create --name rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=prod Application=$WORKLOAD
az group create --name rg-$WORKLOAD-app-prod-eastus --location eastus \
  --tags Environment=prod Application=$WORKLOAD

# 2. Create the spoke VNet
az network vnet create \
  --name vnet-$WORKLOAD-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefixes 10.1.0.0/22

# 3. Create subnets
az network vnet subnet create \
  --name snet-$WORKLOAD-app-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-$WORKLOAD-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.1.0.0/24

az network vnet subnet create \
  --name snet-$WORKLOAD-data-prod-eastus \
  --vnet-name vnet-$WORKLOAD-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus \
  --address-prefix 10.1.1.0/24

# 4. Peer the spoke VNet to the hub
SPOKE_VNET_ID=$(az network vnet show \
  --name vnet-$WORKLOAD-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus \
  --query id -o tsv)

# Hub -> spoke peering (run in hub subscription)
az account set --subscription $HUB_SUB
az network vnet peering create \
  --name peer-hub-to-$WORKLOAD \
  --vnet-name vnet-hub-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-connectivity-prod-eastus \
  --remote-vnet $SPOKE_VNET_ID \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic

# Spoke -> hub peering (run in spoke subscription)
az account set --subscription $APP_SUB
az network vnet peering create \
  --name peer-$WORKLOAD-to-hub \
  --vnet-name vnet-$WORKLOAD-prod-eastus \
  --resource-group rg-$WORKLOAD-network-prod-eastus \
  --remote-vnet $HUB_VNET_ID \
  --allow-vnet-access \
  --allow-forwarded-traffic \
  --use-remote-gateways   # route traffic through hub VPN/ExpressRoute gateway

# 5. Grant the application team Contributor on their subscription
az role assignment create \
  --assignee 'payments-team@contoso.com' \
  --role 'Contributor' \
  --scope "/subscriptions/$APP_SUB"

echo 'Application landing zone for $WORKLOAD provisioned.'
Bicep: Platform Landing Zone Deployment

Production platform landing zone deployments use a modular Bicep structure where a subscription-scope entry template creates resource groups and then calls child modules for the hub VNet, Azure Firewall, and private DNS zones. This allows each component to be updated independently without redeploying the entire platform stack. The hub VNet ID and Firewall private IP are output from the platform template and passed as inputs to each application landing zone deployment.
Bicep: Application Landing Zone Deployment

Each application landing zone is provisioned via a subscription-scope Bicep template called once per workload. It creates the standard resource group set (network, app), deploys a spoke VNet with standardized subnets, creates the spoke-to-hub VNet peering, and grants the application team Contributor on the subscription. The hub-to-spoke peering must be created separately in the hub subscription — this cross-subscription dependency is the only manual coordination step.
Auditing Landing Zone Topology with Resource Graph

Resource Graph queries against the ResourceContainers and Resources tables can verify that every spoke VNet is peered to the hub and that peerings are in the Connected state. These queries form the backbone of a weekly landing zone health report and can detect orphaned subscriptions that were created outside the IaC process.

// Resource Graph: audit landing zone topology

// 1. Find spoke VNets and their peering status to the hub
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks'
| where name startswith 'vnet-'
| where name !startswith 'vnet-hub'
| mv-expand peering = properties.virtualNetworkPeerings
| where isnotempty(peering)
| extend
    peeringName  = tostring(peering.name),
    peeringState = tostring(peering.properties.peeringState),
    remoteVnet   = tostring(peering.properties.remoteVirtualNetwork.id)
| where remoteVnet contains 'vnet-hub'
| project spokeName = name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId, peeringName, peeringState, remoteVnet
| order by peeringState asc, spokeName asc

// 2. Find spoke VNets that are NOT peered to the hub (potential orphaned subscriptions)
Resources
| where type =~ 'microsoft.network/virtualnetworks'
| where name startswith 'vnet-'
| where name !startswith 'vnet-hub'
| extend peerCount = array_length(properties.virtualNetworkPeerings)
| where peerCount == 0 or isnull(peerCount)
| project name, resourceGroup, subscriptionId

// 3. Count resources per application landing zone subscription
Resources
| where type !startswith 'microsoft.resources'
| summarize ResourceCount = count() by subscriptionId
| join kind=leftouter (
    ResourceContainers
    | where type =~ 'microsoft.resources/subscriptions'
    | project subscriptionId = subscriptionId, subName = name
) on subscriptionId
| project subName, subscriptionId, ResourceCount
| order by ResourceCount desc
Baseline Policy Assignments at the Workloads Management Group

The management group that contains all application landing zone subscriptions (mg-workloads) is the right scope for baseline policies that must apply to every workload: centralized diagnostic routing, denial of public IPs that bypass the Firewall, region restrictions, and Defender for Cloud enablement. These policies are assigned once at the MG level and automatically apply to every subscription that joins the group.

# Assign baseline policies to the mg-workloads management group
# These apply to ALL application landing zones automatically

MG_SCOPE='/providers/Microsoft.Management/managementGroups/mg-workloads'

# 1. Require diagnostic settings routed to the central Log Analytics workspace
LOG_WORKSPACE_ID='/subscriptions/<mgmt-sub-id>/resourceGroups/rg-management-prod-eastus/providers/Microsoft.OperationalInsights/workspaces/log-management-prod-eastus'

az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deploy-diagnostic-settings' \
  --display-name 'Deploy diagnostic settings to central Log Analytics workspace' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/6f8f98a4-f108-47cb-8e98-a527f8bf5a75' \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE \
  --params "{\"logAnalytics\": {\"value\": \"$LOG_WORKSPACE_ID\"}}" \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned \
  --location eastus

# 2. Deny creation of public IP addresses (all traffic must go through hub Firewall)
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'deny-public-ip' \
  --display-name 'Deny public IP addresses in application landing zones' \
  --policy '/providers/Microsoft.Authorization/policyDefinitions/83a86a26-fd1f-447c-b59d-e51f44264114' \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE \
  --enforcement-mode Default

# 3. Require resources to be in approved regions
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'allowed-locations' \
  --display-name 'Restrict deployments to approved Azure regions' \
  --policy 'e56962a6-4747-49cd-b67b-bf8b01975c4c' \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE \
  --params "{\"listOfAllowedLocations\": {\"value\": [\"eastus\", \"eastus2\", \"westus2\"]}}"

# 4. Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud on all subscriptions
az policy assignment create \
  --name 'enable-defender' \
  --display-name 'Enable Microsoft Defender for Cloud' \
  --policy-set-definition '1f3afdf9-d0c9-4c3d-847f-89da613e70a8' \
  --scope $MG_SCOPE \
  --identity-type SystemAssigned \
  --location eastus
Landing Zone Patterns Comparison
Pattern
Description
When to use
Hub-spoke (recommended)
Single hub subscription + N spoke subscriptions, all peered. Traffic between spokes routes through the hub Firewall.
Most enterprise environments; provides centralized network security and DNS
Virtual WAN
Microsoft-managed hub (Azure Virtual WAN) replaces customer-managed hub VNet. Spokes connect to the WAN hub.
Large global deployments with >10 regions; simplifies hub management at the cost of flexibility
Flat (single VNet)
All resources share one large VNet with subnets per workload. No separate subscriptions.
Very small organizations (<50 resources); not recommended for growth
Federated platform
Multiple hub subscriptions (one per region or one per business unit) each with their own Firewall and ExpressRoute.
Multi-national orgs with separate network teams per region or strict data residency requirements
Summary

Platform landing zones hold shared infrastructure (hub VNet, Firewall, DNS, identity, management) in dedicated subscriptions owned by the platform team. Application landing zones are individual subscriptions for each workload, containing a spoke VNet peered to the hub and workload-specific resources owned by the application team. The platform team provisions application landing zones and then hands them off; the application team deploys within them. This separation of concerns is the key organizational pattern that lets an enterprise scale to hundreds of subscriptions without the platform team becoming a bottleneck for workload deployments.
